Functions live in networks, not in parts

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

One of the most important shifts in our understanding of human memory began with an apparent contradiction. A man could learn a task and, at the same time, have no memory of having learned it. The experience did not vanish entirely, but it also did not become conscious recollection. Something remained in the gesture, in the performance, in the gradual improvement, even when personal narrative could no longer register what had happened.
That kind of case changed the meaning of memory because it showed that memory is not a single faculty. For a long time, it was intuitive to imagine memory as an internal archive: something happens, the brain records it, the person retrieves it later. But the brain does not seem to work as a central storage room for recollections. It organizes different forms of continuity. There is memory for events, facts, skills, emotions, smells, routes, faces, habits, words, gestures, and bodily states. Some can fail while others remain active.
This is more than a neurological classification. When a person loses the ability to form new conscious memories but still preserves the learning of a skill, the question changes. It is no longer enough to ask where memory is located. We need to ask which kind of memory is operating, through which structures, in which circuit, by which route, and through what form of access.
Memory stops being a thing and becomes an architecture.
This change matters because it reveals a broader principle of the brain. Structures have preferred roles, but they do not own entire functions. The hippocampus is important for forming new declarative memories, but it is not “memory.” The cerebellum participates in motor coordination, but it also appears in adjustment, prediction, and the refinement of action. The basal ganglia participate in habits, reward, and behavioral selection. The prefrontal cortex participates in planning, inhibition, decision, and social regulation. None of these structures works as an isolated part. Each one participates in assemblies.
The mistake is to confuse location with ownership. When we point to an area of the brain and say that a function is there, we simplify a network. The function does not live there. It passes through there. It depends on that region in certain cases, but it is not exhausted by it. Memory, language, emotion, movement, identity, and attention emerge from cooperation between specialized parts, distributed connections, body, context, and history.
This is why the brain is both precise and ambiguous. A small lesion can produce a specific effect. A major alteration can be partially compensated. A person may lose one form of recollection and preserve another. Someone can forget the episode and retain the skill. Someone may be unable to explain a preference and still react to a smell, a song, a place, or an old sensation. Narrative can fail while the organism continues to carry traces of the past.
The body also remembers, though not in the same way a person remembers a date or tells a story. It remembers through gesture, tone, breath, balance, fear, relief, habit, and familiarity. Learning to swim is not merely storing a mental instruction about arms, legs, and air. Swimming distributes itself through motor coordination, respiratory control, proprioception, balance, rhythm, confidence, muscular resistance, circulation, and adaptation to the medium. The skill does not reside in a sentence. It resides in a functional organization of the whole body.
The same happens with riding a bicycle, playing an instrument, handwriting, cooking, walking through a familiar neighborhood, or recognizing the smell of a childhood home. Not everything returns as verbal memory. Some things return as orientation. Others as impulse. Others as refusal. Others as comfort. The brain does not preserve life only through internal images; it distributes life through ways of acting, feeling, predicting, and adjusting.
This distribution makes the system more robust, but also harder to interpret. The human body does not respond like a standardized machine. A drug may work well for most people and produce an adverse reaction in others. A stimulus may organize one person and overload another. A teaching method may open a path for some and block others. A routine may stabilize one system and trap another. The average can guide us, but it does not describe the whole system.
That difference is not a secondary detail. It belongs to the nature of living systems. Every organism carries a history of sensitivities, compensations, limits, preferences, injuries, habits, learning, and contexts. The same signal does not cross every system in the same way. In some, it moves through quick routes. In others, through longer circuits. In others, it meets noise, defense, saturation, fear, pain, or indifference. Treating the average response as if it were the real response is a technical error.
This is where multiple memory stops being only a topic in neuroscience and begins to touch education. If cognition does not operate through a single entry point, why is learning still so often treated as if explanation, repetition, memorization, and evaluation were enough? A student may not understand through the verbal route, but understand through image. They may fail at the symbol and access the idea through the body. They may not respond to abstraction, but reorganize everything when the content finds a concrete situation. They may struggle to narrate what they know, yet demonstrate competence in action.
This does not mean lowering standards. It means increasing precision. Teaching is not forcing information through the same door until it enters. It is recognizing that content may require different routes: language, image, movement, space, rhythm, narrative, object, repetition, interest, bond, real problem, procedure, pause. When one route fails, insisting on it may only intensify the blockage. Sometimes learning requires changing the category of access.
This reading becomes especially important when we speak about cognitive, sensory, or developmental differences. For some people, sounds, lights, textures, changes in routine, or excessive simultaneity are not minor inconveniences, but heavy organizational loads. The issue is not simply that they do not tolerate certain stimuli; it may also be that the environment offers only one form of entry and demands only one form of response. If one path overloads, another may organize. If speech does not work in that moment, perhaps image does. If image is not enough, perhaps body, rhythm, predictability, or specific interest creates passage.
The principle is simple, but its consequences are large: difficulty is not always incapacity; often, it is incompatibility between the required route and the system available at that moment.
The same logic applies to health. The body is not a collection of independent organs. Nutrition, sleep, circulation, muscles, gut, hormones, immune system, breathing, stress, and brain form a regulatory network. The so-called gut-brain axis shows that digestion, microbiota, immunity, mood, and nervous system are not isolated. Physical exercise does not act only on muscles. It modifies circulation, metabolism, sleep, mood, inflammation, brain plasticity, and functional capacity. Organic life is less a sum of parts than a composition of relations.
When a practice involves many layers at once, its benefits also tend to distribute. Swimming, walking, dancing, playing an instrument, cooking, tending plants, learning something new, or living in meaningful contact with others do not activate a single function. They are assemblies. They involve body, attention, memory, perception, emotion, rhythm, planning, correction, and context. Perhaps integrated practices are powerful because they speak the language the organism already uses, the language of networks.
Computing and artificial intelligence are beginning to encounter the same limit. For decades, computers have been organized through a clear separation of functions: processor, memory, storage, peripherals. This architecture is efficient, but it creates bottlenecks when systems need to move too much data between separate places. As tasks become more complex, especially in AI, shared memory, processing near data, specialized accelerators, contextual retrieval, and more distributed architectures become increasingly important.
Perhaps the future of AI does not depend only on larger models. Perhaps it depends on better assemblies between memory, context, tools, verification, adaptation, action, and continuity. A more robust artificial intelligence would not need to store everything in the same way, just as the brain does not store everything in the same way. Facts, experiences, preferences, rules, procedures, corrected errors, limits, and contexts should occupy different layers, with different weights and different forms of retrieval.
The metaphor of the central archive impoverishes both brain and machine. Intelligent systems do not improve merely by accumulating data. They improve when they organize useful relations between different kinds of information, action, and context. More memory is not enough if memory does not know what role it plays. More processing is not enough if the system spends too much energy moving data without understanding its function. More content is not enough without an architecture of use.
At this point, the analogy with Lego pieces or with DNA bases helps clarify the issue. Complexity does not require an infinite number of fundamental components. Four nitrogenous bases sustain the diversity of known life because they enter into sequences, regulations, expressions, and contexts. The value is not only in the piece. It is in the grammar of combination.
Something similar occurs in the brain. Structures, circuits, neurotransmitters, rhythms, bodily states, memories, sensations, environments, and relationships are continuously combined. A function is a recurring assembly. A behavior is an assembly in execution. Learning is an assembly that has stabilized. A difficulty may be blockage, excess, noise, loss, deviation, or incompatibility of assembly. Plasticity is the possibility of reorganizing part of this arrangement without destroying the whole system.
What multiple memory teaches, then, goes beyond memory. It shows that living systems do not preserve continuity by concentrating everything in one place. They preserve continuity by distributing functions across networks capable of recombination. This distribution is not disorder. It is robustness. This plasticity is not lack of structure. It is adaptation. This specialization is not isolation. It is qualified participation in larger assemblies.
Perhaps many of our models remain poor because they keep looking for single parts behind complex functions. We look for “the place” of memory, “the method” of education, “the remedy” of health, “the module” of intelligence, “the processor” of thought, “the cause” of behavior. But life rarely delivers functions in that form. It operates through relations, intensities, thresholds, trajectories, and recombinations.
Intelligence is not where we point because it is not a still object. It appears when different parts enter into sufficient coordination to produce meaning, action, and continuity. It is in the circuit, not only in the component. It is in the assembly, not only in the piece. It is in the capacity to change route when one path fails, to preserve a skill when a recollection is lost, to find an alternative entry when the main door does not open.
The discovery that memory is multiple did not merely correct an idea about the brain. It opened a wider way of thinking about human and artificial systems. Perhaps educating, treating, designing, learning, and creating depend l
